what_it_does: Records a design recommendation that adopts Tim's MSG2 revision of a touch interaction model: a normal tap selects the default content block, while a long-press escalates to finer-grained control and a contextual command bar.
observations: References a revision from Tim in 'MSG2' that prefers tap-to-select and reserves long-hold for 'other actions'
Proposes normal tap = select the default/obvious block and long-press = power-user escalation (level-scrubber and/or contextual command bar)
Frames the model as resolving a collision with native text selection by making taps unambiguous and instant
Explicitly compares the long-press behavior to the iOS UIContextMenu / Home-screen icon pattern
Recommends investing in 'smart' default-block detection so the tap path is correct most of the time
Uses noticeboard frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated timestamps, and history
standouts: Reframes long-press from a primary selection path to a power-user escalation gesture
Explicitly anchors the design in a known platform idiom (iOS UIContextMenu) rather than a custom gesture language
Makes the quality of default-block detection the critical enabler for the tap-first model
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item schema with YAML frontmatter and markdown body
Uses a numbered heading that matches the title and references an external thread/message (MSG2)
Cites a parent item via a part_of link
concerns: Relies on undefined external references (MSG2, Tim, §1 collision, 'smart' block parsing, level-scrubber) without explaining them locally
States a strong recommendation but includes no acceptance criteria, prototype notes, or measurement plan
notes: A design-rationale note arguing for a tap-to-select-default / long-press-to-escalate interaction model, aligned with iOS UIContextMenu conventions.
questions: What is MSG2 and who is Tim in this context?
What exactly is the §1 collision being resolved?
What does the 'level-scrubber' control and how is it triggered?
What constitutes the 'smart' block parsing mentioned as a dependency?
Which application or surface is this interaction model intended for?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to recommend honoring Tim's MSG2 revision as the better architecture, and the body does exactly that by explaining the model and its rationale.
apparent_themes: Reducing friction for the common case while preserving precision
Default detection as a quality multiplier
intention_signals: Advocate for adopting a specific revised touch-interaction architecture and persuade the team to treat Tim's MSG2 proposal as the preferred direction.
novelty: Distinct in explicitly inverting the original gesture priority by making the long-press a secondary escalation path and binding it to an iOS-style context menu / command bar pattern.
contribution: Captures a concrete interaction-model proposal (tap commits default block; long-press opens scrubber/command bar), its rationale for avoiding native-selection conflicts, and its dependency on reliable default-block parsing.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note recommending a tap-to-select-default, long-press-to-escalate interaction model inspired by iOS UIContextMenu, with strong default-block detection as a prerequisite.
intention_for_embedding: To persuade the team to adopt Tim's MSG2 revision as the canonical touch interaction architecture.
